topics.label: Bragg's Equation
topics.note: n = order of diffraction, \(\lambda\) = wavelength of X-ray, \(\theta\) = angle of diffraction, d = interplanar distance

topics.label: Packing Efficiency
topics.latex: \text{Packing Efficiency} = \dfrac{\text{Volume occupied by atoms}}{\text{Total volume of unit cell}} \times 100
topics.note: BCC = 68% | Simple Cubic = 52.4% | HCP & CCP (FCC) = 74%

topics.label: Density of Unit Cell
topics.note: Z = atoms/unit cell, M = molar mass, a = edge length, \(N_A\) = Avogadro number

items.text: If N = close packed spheres → tetrahedral voids = 2N
Octahedral voids = N

topics.label: Schottky Defect
topics.text: Equal number of cations and anions missing from lattice sites. Common in high coordination number ionic compounds (NaCl, KCl, KBr, CsCl, AgBr). Density decreases.

topics.label: Frenkel Defect
topics.text: Cation displaced from lattice to interstitial site. Common in low coordination number ionic compounds (AgBr, AgCl, ZnS). Density unchanged.

topics.label: F-centres
topics.text: Metal excess defect — anion vacancy sites occupied by electrons = F-centres. Responsible for colour & paramagnetism.

items.text: Doping Si/Ge with Group VA (P or As). 5th electron conducts electricity.
Doping Si/Ge with Group IIIA (B, Al, Ga, In). Electron vacancy (hole) conducts.
Strongly attracted; retain magnetism. All electrons aligned same direction. Eg: Fe, Co, Ni
Weakly attracted; due to unpaired electrons. Eg: Cu²⁺, Fe³⁺, Na, Cr³⁺
Domains aligned parallel & antiparallel in unequal numbers. Eg: Fe₃O₄, MgFe₂O₄
Equal parallel & antiparallel domains → net moment = 0. Eg: MnO, MnO₂

topics.label: Molarity
topics.note: Units: mol L⁻¹

topics.label: Molality
topics.note: Units: mol kg⁻¹

topics.label: Raoult's Law — Relative Lowering of VP
topics.note: \(P^0\) = VP of pure solvent, \(P_s\) = VP of solution

topics.label: Henry's Law
topics.note: Partial pressure of dissolved gas ∝ its mole fraction in solution

topics.label: Depression in Freezing Point
topics.note: \(K_f\) = cryoscopic constant (K·kg·mol⁻¹), \(W_1\) = mass of solvent (g), M = molar mass of solute

topics.label: Elevation in Boiling Point
topics.note: \(K_b\) = ebullioscopic constant (K·kg·mol⁻¹)

items.text: VP > predicted. \(\Delta H_{mix} > 0\), \(\Delta V_{mix} > 0\). Eg: Ethanol + water, Acetone + CS₂
VP < predicted. \(\Delta H_{mix} < 0\), \(\Delta V_{mix} < 0\). Eg: HNO₃ + water, Chloroform + Acetone
Obeys Raoult's law. \(\Delta H_{mix} = 0\), \(\Delta V_{mix} = 0\). Eg: Benzene + Toluene, n-hexane + n-heptane

topics.label: Rate of Reaction
topics.text: Decrease in concentration of reactant (or increase in product concentration) per unit time. Units: mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹

topics.label: Rate Constant — nth Order Units
topics.note: Zero order: mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | First order: s⁻¹ | Second order: mol⁻¹·L·s⁻¹

topics.label: Arrhenius Equation
topics.note: A = frequency factor, \(E_a\) = activation energy (J/mol), R = 8.314 J/mol·K

topics.label: Activation Energy
topics.note: Catalyst lowers activation energy. Temperature increase → rate almost doubles per 10°C rise.

topics.label: Galvanic (Daniell) Cell
topics.text: Zn rod in ZnSO₄ = Anode (−ve, oxidation). Cu rod in CuSO₄ = Cathode (+ve, reduction). Salt bridge connects internally; metallic wire externally.

topics.label: EMF of Cell
topics.note: Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE/NHE) potential = 0 V (reference)

topics.label: Gibbs Energy & EMF
topics.note: F = Faraday constant = 96500 C mol⁻¹

topics.label: Cell Constant
topics.note: l = distance between electrodes, A = cross-section area of electrode

topics.label: Molar Conductivity
topics.note: Units: S·cm²·mol⁻¹

topics.label: Kohlrausch's Law
topics.note: Equivalent conductance of ion ∝ mobility of ion

topics.label: Faraday's First Law
topics.note: m = mass deposited, e = electrochemical equivalent, Q = charge (C), I = current (A), t = time (s)

topics.label: Faraday's Second Law
topics.note: Same quantity of electricity → masses deposited ∝ equivalent weights

items.text: Non-rechargeable. Eg: Dry cell, Mercury cell
Rechargeable (charging = electrolytic; discharging = electrochemical). Eg: Lead storage battery
Chemical energy of fuel → electrical energy directly. Eg: H₂–O₂ fuel cell
Weak, reversible, fast. ΔH = 20–40 kJ/mol. Low activation energy.
Strong, irreversible, slow. ΔH = 80–240 kJ/mol. High activation energy.

topics.label: Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm
topics.note: Log form: \(\log\dfrac{x}{m} = \log k + \dfrac{1}{n}\log p\). Slope = 1/n, Intercept = log k

items.text: Solvent-loving. Eg: starch, protein, polymers
Solvent-hating. Eg: gold sol, metal sulphides, metal oxides
Scattering of light by colloidal particles; illuminated path = Tyndall cone
Continuous zig-zag motion of colloidal particles due to unequal bombardment
Movement of colloidal particles under applied EMF
Movement of dispersion medium when colloidal particles are held stationary
Colloidal solution → precipitate on adding electrolyte

topics.label: Hardy-Schulze Rule
topics.text: Greater the charge of the coagulating ion, greater its coagulating power. Anions: Cl⁻ < SO₄²⁻ < PO₄³⁻ < [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻. Cations: Na⁺ < Mg²⁺ < Al³⁺

topics.label: Gold Number
topics.text: Mass (mg) of protective colloid preventing coagulation of 10 mL gold sol on adding 1 mL of 10% NaCl. Gelatin = 0.005–0.01 (smaller = better protective colloid)

items.text: Catalyst & reactants in same phase
Different phases. Eg: \(2SO_2 + O_2 \xrightarrow{V_2O_5} 2SO_3\)
Product acts as catalyst. Eg: \(2AsH_3 \rightarrow 2As + 3H_2\), As is autocatalyst
Substance that enhances catalyst activity. Eg: Mo in Haber's process (Fe catalyst)
Decreases catalyst activity. Eg: CO is poison for Fe in Haber's process
Naturally occurring inorganic substance of definite chemical composition
Mineral from which metal can be extracted conveniently & economically
Added to remove gangue. Acidic: SiO₂, P₄O₁₀. Basic: CaO, MgO. Lowers melting point.
Impurities associated with ore
Gangue + flux → fusible product. Eg: FeO + SiO₂ → FeSiO₃
Molten Cu₂S + little FeS (intermediate in Cu extraction)
Heating in excess air/O₂ below fusion temp. \(2ZnS + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2ZnO + 2SO_2\)
Heating in limited/no air below fusion temp. Carbonate → oxide. \(CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaO + CO_2\)
Molten metal stirred with green wood poles; hydrocarbons reduce oxides. Eg: Cu, Sn → 99.5% pure
Impure metal = anode, pure strip = cathode, metal salt = electrolyte. Impurities settle as anode mud. Eg: Cu, Ag, Au
Based on different solubility of impurities in molten vs solid metal. Eg: Si, Ge, Ga
Metal → volatile compound → decomposed to pure metal. Mond's process (Ni), Van Arkel method (Zr, Ti)

topics.label: Hall-Héroult Process (Aluminium)
topics.text: Electrolyte: Alumina + Cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) + Fluorspar (CaF₂). Cathode: iron box with gas carbon lining. Anode: graphite rods. Cryolite → makes alumina good conductor. Fluorspar → lowers fusion temp from 2323 K to ~1140 K.

items.text: Diatomic (N≡N), triple bond, bond dissociation energy = 945 kJ mol⁻¹
Tetratomic (P₄), P–P single bonds, bond dissociation energy = 213 kJ mol⁻¹
Reluctance of ns² electrons to participate in bonding; stability of +5 state ↓ down the group; Bi most stable in +3
NH₃ > PH₃ > AsH₃ > SbH₃ (central atom size ↑ → electronegativity ↓ → repulsion ↓)

topics.label: Haber's Process — NH₃
topics.note: Conditions: 725–775 K, 200 atm, Fe catalyst + Mo (promoter). Cannot be dried with H₂SO₄, CaCl₂, or P₄O₁₀.

topics.label: White vs Red Phosphorus
rows: Insoluble in H₂O, soluble in CS₂

items.text: Trigonal bipyramidal; equatorial bonds (220 pm) < axial bonds (240 pm). Ionic in solid state: [PCl₄]⁺[PCl₆]⁻
Triprotic, OS of P = +5
Diprotic (2 ionisable H), OS of P = +3
Monoprotic (1 ionisable H), OS = +1, strong reducing agent
NO, N₂O
P₄O₆, P₄O₁₀

items.sub: OS of N in HNO₃ and N₂O₅
Iron doesn't dissolve in conc. HNO₃
items.text: Oxide film forms → metal becomes passive
Small size, high electronegativity → pπ–pπ multiple bonds → diatomic
Bigger size, lower electronegativity → S–S single bonds, high catenation
Oxygen — 46.6% by weight of earth's crust
H₂O — strong intermolecular H-bonding
H₂Te
First four elements called ore-forming (chalcogens). Highest EN: O. Highest electron gain enthalpy: S

topics.label: Preparation of Ozone
topics.note: Cold, dry O₂ stream; up to 10% conversion only

items.text: Turns starch-iodide paper blue; turns alcoholic benzidine solution brown; Hg loses meniscus
Yellow, MP = 385.8 K, SG = 2.06, soluble in CS₂. Stable below 369 K
Yellow, MP = 392 K, SG = 1.98, soluble in CS₂. Stable above 369 K
369 K: α-S ⇌ β-S. Both have S₈ molecules (puckered octagonal ring)

topics.label: O₂ & O₃ Magnetic Nature
topics.text: O₂ is paramagnetic (2 unpaired electrons — MO theory). O₃ is diamagnetic (all electrons paired). In vapour state, S partially exists as S₂ (like O₂) and is paramagnetic.

items.text: OS of O = +2
OS of O = +1
OS of O = −1 (peroxide)
OS of O = −1/2 (superoxide)

topics.label: CaO as Drying Agent
topics.text: CaO (quick lime) is used for drying ammonia gas. H₂SO₄, anhydrous CaCl₂, and P₄O₁₀ cannot be used — they react with NH₃.

topics.label: H₂SO₄ + CaF₂ Reaction
topics.note: Method of preparing HF in lab

items.latex: HCl < HBr < HI < HF \;\;(\text{HF highest due to H-bonding})

items.text: Cl > F — F has small size → strong inter-electron repulsion when electron is added
Chlorides, bromides, iodides of Na, K, Mg, Ca
Bleaches by reduction; original colour can be restored on exposure to air
Cl₂ and SO₂ act as bleaching agents only in presence of moisture
+1, +3, +5, +7 respectively
More O atoms on conjugate base → greater dispersal of negative charge → greater stability → stronger acid
AX, AX₃, AX₅, AX₇ (A = larger halogen, X = smaller; OS of X = −1 always)
sp³d hybridisation, 3 bp + 2 lp
sp³d² hybridisation, 5 bp + 1 lp
sp³d³ hybridisation, 7 bp + 0 lp
sp³d hybridisation, 2 bp + 3 lp
Small size, highest electronegativity, low F–F bond energy, no d-orbitals in valency shell
HOF (hypofluorous acid); others form multiple oxoacids
Small size → strong inter-electron repulsion when adding an electron
HCl, HBr, HI are gases — HF has H-bonding
ns²np⁶ (stable); exception: He = 1s²
Argon
Radon — obtained from ²²⁶Ra decay
Helium
Xenon
Radon
Xe[PtF₆] — prepared by Neil Bartlett (1962)
sp³d, 2 bp + 3 lp → Linear
sp³d², 4 bp + 2 lp → Square planar
sp³d³, 6 bp + 1 lp → Distorted octahedral
sp³d², 5 bp + 1 lp → Square pyramidal, bond angle = 90°
sp³, 3 bp + 1 lp → Pyramidal, bond angle = 103°
Discharge tubes, fluorescent bulbs, advertisement & display signs, botanical gardens
Inert atmosphere in high-temp metallurgy; filling electric bulbs; handling air-sensitive substances in labs
Diving apparatus (O₂+He mix; He has low solubility in blood vs N₂); meteorological balloons (non-inflammable)
Radiotherapy (cancer treatment)
Elements with at least one unpaired d-electron in atom or common ion with common oxidation states
Zn, Cd, Hg — d-block but NOT transition elements (completely filled d¹⁰ in atom and common ions)
Properties transition between s-block and p-block elements
Less energy difference between ns and (n−1)d sub-shells; both participate in bonding
d–d transitions when d-orbitals split in crystal field
Ability to adopt multiple oxidation states and form complexes
Availability of empty d-orbitals for coordinate bond formation
Similar atomic radii of transition metals

items.latex: Mn^{2+}\text{ — unpaired electrons → paramagnetic; }Zn^{2+}\text{ — no unpaired e → diamagnetic}

items.text: 6 ligands surround metal ion; repulsion between ligand lone pairs and d-orbitals

items.latex: \Delta_o > P \text{ (pairing energy) → low spin (inner orbital complex)}

items.text: Empty d-orbitals (Sc³⁺) or completely filled d-orbitals (Cu⁺, Zn²⁺) — no d–d transition

topics.label: Spectrochemical Series
topics.text: Ligands arranged in increasing crystal field strength (increasing CFSE/Δo): I⁻ < Br⁻ < SCN⁻ < Cl⁻ < F⁻ < OH⁻ < C₂O₄²⁻ < H₂O < NCS⁻ < NH₃ < en < CN⁻ < CO. Larger Δo → greater stability of complex.

topics.label: Spin-only Magnetic Moment
topics.note: n = number of unpaired electrons

items.text: Gradual decrease in atomic and ionic sizes of lanthanoids from left to right
Poor shielding effect of f-orbitals → effective nuclear charge increases across the series
Similarity in properties of 2nd and 3rd transition series (e.g., Zr & Hf, Nb & Ta, Mo & W)
Separation of lanthanoids is difficult due to similar sizes
Positive ions named first, then negative ions
2=di, 3=tri, 4=tetra; for complex ligands: bis, tris, tetrakis
End with suffix '-o' (e.g., chloro, cyano, hydroxo)
Named as-is; exceptions: H₂O=aqua, NH₃=ammine, CO=carbonyl, NO=nitrosyl
Tetrahydroxozincate(II) ion
Hexaamminecobalt(III) sulphate
Potassium tris(oxalato)chromate(III)
Hexaamminecobalt(III) chloride
Tetracarbonylnickel(0) — tetrahedral
Pentacarbonyliron(0) — trigonal bipyramidal
Chemical species (neutral/anion/cation) that donates electron pairs to form coordinate bond with central metal
Number of donor atoms of ligands directly bonded to central metal
Bidentate or polydentate ligand forming a closed/cyclic structure. Eg: ethylenediamine (en), EDTA
Has two donor atoms but only one bonds at a time. Eg: NO₂⁻ (nitro/nitrito), SCN⁻ (thiocyanato/isothiocyanato)
= oxidation state of central atom; ionisable, non-directional; shown by dotted lines
= coordination number; non-ionisable, directional; shown by thick lines
Central metal + directly attached ligands, enclosed in square brackets [ ]

Types Ma₄b₂, Ma₃b₃

Eg: Polythene, Buna-N, Buna-S, PVC, Teflon, Polystyrene
Eg: Nylon-6,6, Dacron, Bakelite, Urea-formaldehyde
Eg: Bakelite, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine

Two or more monomer types. Eg: Nylon-6,6, Buna-N, Buna-S
Weakest intermolecular forces → can be stretched. Eg: Natural rubber, Buna-S, Buna-N
Strongest forces (H-bonding/dipole-dipole). Eg: Polyamides, Polyesters
Linear/slightly branched; softens on heating. Eg: Polythene, PVC, Teflon

Cis-polyisoprene; cis configuration at every double bond

Vulcanised rubber-like polymer stretchable to 2× its length and returns to original shape
TiCl₄ + Al(C₂H₅)₃ — used in stereoregular polymerisation
Poly-β-hydroxybutyrate-co-β-hydroxyvalerate — used in specialty packaging, orthopaedic devices, capsule outer covering
Benzoyl peroxide — common initiator in free radical polymerisation
Reduce Fehling's solution & Tollens' reagent. Eg: All monosaccharides, Lactose, Maltose
Cannot reduce Fehling's/Tollens'. Eg: Sucrose

Also called milk sugar
Spontaneous change in specific rotation of an optically active compound
Stereoisomers differing in arrangement of −H and −OH at C₁ (aldose) or C₂ (ketose). Eg: α-D-glucopyranose & β-D-glucopyranose

items.latex: \alpha(C_1 - C_4)\text{ glycosidic linkage between glucose units}

Hydrolysis gives two α-D-glucose molecules
Contains both −NH₂ and −COOH functional groups in same molecule
Polypeptide with >100 amino acid residues, molecular mass >10,000 u. Eg: Hair, silk, insulin
Ion with +ve charge at one end and −ve charge at other end
−CO−NH− (amide linkage) between two amino acids
Cannot be synthesised by body; taken through diet. Eg: Valine, Leucine
Can be synthesised by body. Eg: Glycine, Alanine

Chains run parallel; held by H-bonds and disulphide bonds
Breaking of organised protein structure → loss of biological activity
Nitrogenous base + Pentose sugar
Nitrogenous base + Pentose sugar + Phosphate ion
RNA contains ribose sugar and uracil instead of deoxyribose and thymine
Thymine (T) — found only in DNA
Guanine and cytosine present in both DNA and RNA. Pyrimidine in both: cytosine
Held by hydrogen bonding between complementary bases (A−T, G−C)
Duplication of single DNA molecule into two identical daughter DNA molecules
Eg: Testosterone, Estrogen, Progesterone
Eg: Insulin, Oxytocin
Eg: Thyroxine
Decreases blood glucose level; example of protein hormone
Helps implantation of fertilised egg
Controls menstrual cycle
Characterised by lethargy and obesity
Control carbohydrate metabolism and regulate inflammatory reactions
Low MW chemicals (100–500 u) that react with macromolecular targets to produce biological response
Drugs useful in diagnosis, prevention and treatment of diseases. Eg: Paracetamol, Aspirin, Ibuprofen
For treatment of stress and mental disease. Eg: Diazepam (Valium), Veronal
Reduce pain without disturbing nervous system. Eg: Aspirin, Ibuprofen
Relieve pain and produce sleep; addictive. Eg: Morphine, Heroin, Codeine
No addictive properties. Eg: Aspirin, Ibuprofen
Reduce high fever and relieve mild pain. Eg: Analgin, Paracetamol
Produced by microorganisms; inhibit growth of other microorganisms. Eg: Penicillin, Streptomycin, Tetracycline, Chloramphenicol
Remove excess acid in stomach and normalise pH. Eg: Mg(OH)₂, Al(OH)₃, NaHCO₃
Prevent histamine interaction with stomach receptors. Eg: Ranitidine (Zintac), Bromopheniramine
Oral contraceptives (steroids). Eg: Norethindrone
Kill/resist microorganism growth on living tissues. Eg: Dettol, Iodine, Iodoform, Boric acid, Furacine
Mixture of chloroxylenol + terpineol
2–3% iodine in alcohol-water solution
Added to soap to impart antiseptic properties
Kill microorganisms on non-living surfaces. Eg: Formalin, 1% phenol, Cl₂ at 0.2–0.4 ppm in water, SO₂ in low concentration
Kill very small microorganisms (bacteria, virus). Eg: Lysozyme, Lactic acid, HCl in stomach
100× sweeter than sucrose
550× sweeter than sucrose
600× sweeter than sucrose
2000× sweeter than sucrose

Sodium salts of sulphonated long chain alcohols or hydrocarbons. Eg: Sodium lauryl sulphate: CH₃(CH₂)₁₁CH₂OSO₃Na⁺

CTAB — Cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide

topics.label: Ambident Nucleophiles
topics.text: Groups with two nucleophilic centres are called ambident nucleophiles. Eg: CN⁻ (can attack via C or N), NO₂⁻ (can attack via N giving nitro, or O giving nitrito).

items.text: (1) Protonation of alkene → carbocation (2) Nucleophilic attack of H₂O (3) Deprotonation

Aldehyde → primary alcohol; Ketone → secondary alcohol

Due to intermolecular H-bonding (not present in ethers and hydrocarbons)
